title: Hot Take - Law Schools Are Failing Their Students
subject: Hot Take: Law schools are producing graduates who can't work with AI. That's malpractice-adjacent.
preheader: 3 years and $200K for a degree that doesn't include the most important skill of the decade.
bodyHtml:
LegalTech AI Hub

Hot Take

Law Schools Are Producing AI-Illiterate Graduates. That’s Educational Malpractice.

A law student graduating in May 2026 has spent three years and roughly $200,000 learning to practice law in a world that no longer exists. Most of them have never been taught how to evaluate an AI tool’s output, build a verification workflow, or understand the ethical obligations that come with AI-assisted practice. And in six months, they’ll be expected to do all of these things.

This is a curriculum failure, and it’s indefensible.

Let me be specific about what I mean. I’m not saying law schools need to teach students to code. I’m not saying every 1L should take a machine learning course. I’m saying that a law school that doesn’t teach its students how to work alongside AI tools responsibly is failing those students the same way a medical school would fail by not teaching residents how to use diagnostic imaging.

Here’s what every law graduate should know by 2027:

1. How to verify AI output. Not “check if it sounds right,” but a systematic verification process. Prompt, review, cross-reference, document. This should be as ingrained as Bluebook citation.

2. When AI is appropriate and when it isn’t. Using AI to draft a first pass of a research memo? Fine. Using AI to evaluate witness credibility? Ethically and practically problematic. Students need frameworks for these decisions.

3. The ethical landscape. Disclosure obligations, confidentiality risks, competence requirements, unauthorized practice implications. These aren’t future hypotheticals — they’re current professional obligations that vary by jurisdiction.

4. Prompt engineering for legal tasks. The difference between a vague prompt and a structured legal prompt is the difference between useless output and genuinely useful output. This is a trainable skill that takes about 10 hours to develop basic competency in.

The counterargument I hear most often from law professors: “We teach students to think like lawyers. The technology will change; the thinking won’t.” This sounds wise until you realize it’s the same argument medical schools could have made about teaching anatomy instead of radiology. Yes, foundational skills matter. But competence in the tools of the profession isn’t optional. It’s part of the foundation now.

The stakes are real. A new associate who doesn’t know how to verify AI research will cite a hallucinated case. A new associate who doesn’t understand AI confidentiality risks will paste client data into a public API. A new associate who can’t use AI efficiently will take three times as long as their peers. These aren’t theoretical risks — they’re happening right now at firms across the country.

A handful of schools are getting it right — Stanford, Georgetown, Michigan, Suffolk. But the majority of the 200 ABA-accredited law schools are still teaching as if it’s 2019. Their graduates are entering a 2026 job market with 2019 skills.

If you’re a law school dean reading this: add AI competency to your required curriculum. Not as an elective. Not as a “technology law” course about regulating AI. A practical skills course about using AI in legal practice. Your students’ careers depend on it. So does the profession.

LAW STUDENTS AND PROFESSORS: I WANT TO HEAR FROM YOU

Is your school teaching AI skills? Are you a recent graduate who felt unprepared? Hit reply. Anonymous is fine. I’m writing a longer piece on this and I want real stories.
